Fig. 4. Comparison of predicted and ground truth 𝑣𝑦 trajectories on LVMS data. Ground truth is shown in blue, DDM shown in red, and LE-PAVD model in green.
Fig. 5. Comparison of predicted and ground truth 𝜔 trajectories on LVMS data. Ground truth is shown in blue, DDM shown in red, and LE-PAVD model in green.

Fig. 6. Closed-loop NMPC race trajectories on the ETHZMobil track using model parameters learned from the ETHZ dataset. The dashed line denotes the reference raceline, DDM (red), INA is brown, and the proposed LE-PAVD (green).
TABLE I State, Control, and Dynamics Parameters Used in the LE-PAVD

Fig. 8. Internally estimated tire force components during closed-loop NMPC run on the ETHZMobil track for DDM (red), INA in brown and LE-PAVD (green). Tire forces are model-internal quantities used by the controller, as no direct force measurements are available. LE-PAVD exhibits smoother and more structured force evolution under aggressive maneuvers.
Fig. 7. Comparison for 𝑣𝑥 , 𝑣𝑦 , and 𝜔 on ETHZMobil track MPC run. Deep Dynamics Model (DDM) in red, INA in brown and LE-PAVD model in green.

Fig. 2. LE-PAVD architecture. Sequential feedback and commanded control inputs are processed by a GRU and dense layers to predict next-step states [𝑣𝑥 , 𝑣𝑦 , 𝜔 ]⊤. A Physics Guard Layer enforces parameter bounds and computes interpretable dynamics through slip angles, normal loads, and tire/longitudinal forces, and accelerations while capturing rate-limited actuator behavior.
